nativeRange.habitat: Still to slow-moving rivers, floodplains, lakes, and swampy areas; typically found near dense marginal or submerged vegetation used as ambush cover; prefers warm, often slightly acidic to neutral waters

decorPreferences: large driftwood and root tangles for cover
dense marginal planting (Vallisneria, Sagittaria) along tank walls

diet.notes: Obligate ambush piscivore. In the wild feeds almost exclusively on fish. In captivity typically requires live feeder fish initially; some specimens can be transitioned to large frozen or fresh fish chunks (tilapia fillet, smelt, silverside), frozen whole fish, or large frozen prawns with patient target-feeding. Refuses dry foods in the vast majority of cases. Feeder fish should be gut-loaded and ideally quarantined before use to minimise disease introduction. Never feed live goldfish (fatty, disease vector); use live guppies, rosy red minnows, or similar. Feeding frequency: 2–3 times per week is sufficient for adults.

avoidWith: any fish small enough to be swallowed (up to ~40 cm)
other large predatory fish (high aggression risk)
any fish occupying the same swim zone

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Hepsetus odoe. Verify availability and license before use.

summary: A large Sub-Saharan ambush piscivore reaching 50 cm, with a torpedo-shaped body and massive tooth-filled jaws. Monotypic family Hepsetidae; now recognised as a species complex (H. odoe sensu stricto is West African). A specialist-only species requiring 500 L+, live or large-frozen fish diet, and a species-only setup.
careNotes:
Taxonomic note: Hepsetus odoe was long treated as a single widespread species across Sub-Saharan Africa, but molecular and morphological work by Decru et al. (2012–2013) established that the ‘H. odoe complex’ comprises multiple cryptic species: H. odoe sensu stricto (West Africa: Senegal–Nigeria), H. cuvieri (Congo basin), H. lineatus (East Africa), and H. occidentalis (Upper Niger), among others. Specimens sold in the aquarium trade may belong to any of these species; exact identification from photographs alone is unreliable. Hepsetidae is a monotypic family (single genus, currently ~5 described species) that is the sister group to Citharinidae within Characiformes — a phylogenetically isolated African clade with no close trade relatives.

The African Pike Characin is a large, powerfully built ambush predator with an elongated, laterally compressed body, a large underslung jaw densely armed with canine-like teeth, and prominent lateral-line scales adapted for detecting prey movement. Adults commonly reach 40–50 cm TL in the wild. In captivity, 35–45 cm is more typical, though 50 cm specimens have been recorded.

Tank requirements: A minimum of 500 L with a footprint of at least 180 × 60 cm is essential for a single adult. The species is an explosive burst-swimmer and needs a long, unobstructed mid-water corridor. Dense cover (large driftwood tangles, robust rooted plants along the perimeter) provides security and simulates its ambush niche. Floating plants to reduce surface glare are beneficial. Lighting should be subdued — bright conditions cause chronic stress. A secure, weighted lid is mandatory: this species is a powerful jumper and will exploit any gap.

Water: Tolerates a range of soft-to-moderately-hard, slightly acidic to neutral water. Keep temperature stable at 24–27°C. Ammonia and nitrite must be zero; high organic load (from heavy feeding on whole fish) demands efficient filtration — oversized external canister filters or sump systems are recommended. Large regular water changes (30–40% weekly) are essential to manage waste from a large piscivore diet.

Diet and feeding: This is one of the most challenging aspects of keeping this species. Wild fish are exclusively piscivorous ambush hunters. Newly acquired specimens almost always require live fish to trigger a feeding response. Once feeding confidently on live prey, patient and persistent target-feeding (using tongs or a feeding stick) can occasionally coax some individuals onto large frozen whole fish, frozen smelt, or fresh fish fillet. Many specimens never make this transition and must be maintained on live or fresh fish throughout their lives. Never use goldfish as feeders (high fat content, thiaminase, disease risk); suitable live feeders include gut-loaded guppies, rosy red minnows, or species-appropriate small cyprinids. Feed adults 2–3 times per week; overfeeding accelerates waste load and shortens lifespan.

Compatibility: This is a species-only fish. Any tankmate small enough to fit in its jaws will be eaten — and this fish’s gape is deceptively large. Even sizeable fish are at risk; adults have been documented predating prey nearly half their own body length. Conspecific housing is generally not recommended: dominance fights result in serious injury or death. If attempted in very large systems (1000 L+), provide multiple large visual barriers.

Availability: Rarely available in the hobby; specimens are occasionally exported from West or Central Africa as bycatch of larger fish shipments. Long-term captive success is uncommon, making this a true specialist’s species.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity has not been reliably documented for hobby aquaria. In the wild, Hepsetus odoe spawns in flooded marginal vegetation during the rainy season; the female constructs a floating foam nest anchored to vegetation and the male guards the eggs. Breeding in captivity would require a very large tank, seasonal simulation (temperature drop followed by raise, increased water level), and a mated pair — identifying sexes is difficult outside of breeding condition. Considered not achievable in standard home aquaria.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Hepsetus odoe
primary.notes: Primary source for water parameters, adult size, tank requirements, diet, compatibility, and captive care notes. SeriouslyFish provides the most comprehensive hobbyist-oriented account for this species.

additional.name: FishBase — Hepsetus odoe
additional.notes: Cross-referenced for adult size (max 50 cm TL), native range, taxonomy (family Hepsetidae, order Characiformes), temperature range, and habitat description. Also consulted for the species-complex note arising from Decru et al. 2012–2013 revision.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — African Pike Characin
additional.notes: Used for hobbyist care details including feeding transition strategies, escape risk, and species-only tank recommendation. Supported 500 L minimum tank guidance and noted rarity in the hobby.

additional.name: Decru E. et al. (2012) — Hepsetus species complex revision
additional.notes: Taxonomic reference for the multi-species revision of the H. odoe complex (Decru et al. 2012–2013). Documents H. odoe sensu stricto (West Africa), H. cuvieri (Congo), H. lineatus, and H. occidentalis as distinct species.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Large African Predators in the Aquarium
additional.notes: Supplementary source for captive husbandry context, foam nest breeding behaviour description, and diet/feeding difficulty confirmation in the hobby setting.

fish.breedingNotes: Foam-nest spawner in flooded vegetation; pair bond with male nest guarding in the wild. Not reliably bred in captivity; no documented successful home-aquarium breeding on record.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Reaches ~50cm; ambush piscivore demanding live or large-frozen feeder fish; requires 500L+ species-only or single-large-fish setup. Rare in trade and rarely successfully kept long-term in home aquaria.
